subject: Chemistry
solution:
Given $-\frac{d \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}}{d t}=4.56 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{Ms}^{-1}$
From the reaction given,
$\begin{aligned}
& -\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{dMnO}_4^{-}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{4.56 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \mathrm{Ms}^{-1} \\
& -\frac{1}{2} \frac{d \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}}{d t}=\frac{1}{5} \frac{d \mathrm{I}_2}{d t} \\
& \therefore-\frac{5}{2} \frac{d \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}}{d t}=\frac{d \mathrm{I}_2}{d t}
\end{aligned}$
On substituting the given value
$\therefore \frac{d \mathrm{I}_2}{d t}=\frac{4.56 \times 10^{-3} \times 5}{2}=1.14 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{M} / \mathrm{s}$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
For first-order decomposition with rate equation $[A] = [A]_0 e^{-kt}$, the time for concentration to reduce to fraction f is: $t = \frac{1}{k}\ln\frac{1}{f} = \frac{\ln(1/f)}{k}$
For reduction to 1/8: $t_{1/8} = \frac{\ln 8}{k} = \frac{3\ln 2}{k}$
For reduction to 1/10: $t_{1/10} = \frac{\ln 10}{k}$
Therefore: $\frac{t_{1/8}}{t_{1/10}} = \frac{\ln 8}{\ln 10} = \frac{3\log 2}{\log 10} = \frac{3 \times 0.3}{1} = 0.9$
Thus: $\frac{t_{1/8}}{t_{1/10}} \times 10 = 0.9 \times 10 = 9$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
For a first-order reaction, the rate constant $k$ is given by:

$k = \dfrac{2.303 \log 2}{t_{1/2}}$

Using the standard approximation $2.303 \log 2 \approx 0.693$:

$k = \dfrac{0.693}{6.93} = 0.1 \text{ min}^{-1}$

The time required for $99\%$ completion of the reaction is given by the integrated rate law:

$t = \dfrac{2.303}{k} \log \left( \dfrac{a}{a - x} \right)$

Here, the initial concentration $a = 100$ and the amount reacted $x = 99$, so $a - x = 1$.

$t = \dfrac{2.303}{0.1} \log \left( \dfrac{100}{1} \right)$

$t = 23.03 \times \log(10^2)$

$t = 23.03 \times 2 = 46.06 \text{ minutes}$

Answer: $46.06$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
$A \longrightarrow B$
Initial concentration Rate of reaction
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M} & 2.40 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{Ms}^{-1} \\
1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{M} & 0.60 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{Ms}^{-1}
\end{array}
$$
rate of reaction
$$
r=k[A]^x
$$
where $x=$ order of reaction hence
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2.40 \times 10^{-4}=k\left[2 \times 10^{-3}\right]^x \\
& 0.60 \times 10^{-4}=k\left[1 \times 10^{-3}\right]^x
\end{aligned}
$$
on dividing eqn.(i) from eqn. (ii) we get $4=(2)^x$
$\therefore \quad x=2$
i.e. order of reaction $=2$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
For $A \xrightarrow{K_1} B$
$\ln(2) = \dfrac{E_{a_1}}{R} \left[ \dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{500} \right]$
$E_{a_1} = \dfrac{\ln 2 \times R \times 1500}{2}$
$E_{a_2} = \dfrac{E_{a_1}}{2} = \dfrac{\ln 2 \times R \times 1500}{4}$
$(K_1)_{\text{at 500 K}} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{2}$
$(K_2)_{\text{at 500 K}} = \ln 2$
Now for $C \xrightarrow{K_2} D$
$\ln \left[ \dfrac{(K_2)_{\text{at 500K}}}{(K_2)_{\text{at 300K}}} \right] = \left( \dfrac{\ln 2 \times R \times 1500}{4} \right) \times \dfrac{1}{R} \times \left[ \dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{500} \right]$
$(K_2)_{\text{at 300 K}} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.49$
$(K_2)_{\text{at 300 K}} = 4.9 \times 10^{-1}$.
So, answer is 5.

x = 80

From (i) and (iii)

subject: Chemistry
solution:
If K is known at two different temperatures the activation energy can be calculated as:

At temperature 1: ln K1 = -EaRT1 + ln A

At temperature 2: ln K2 = -EaRT2 + ln A

We can subtract one of these equations from the other:

ln K1 - ln K2 = -EaRT1 + ln A - -EaRT2 + ln A

This equation can further simplified to:

lnK1K2 = EaR1T2 - 1T1 

ln 0.050.03=Ea2.305×8.314×1200-1300

⇒ 0.22 =Ea2.305×8.314×1600

⇒ Ea = 2.3 × 8.3 × 600 × 0.22

⇒Ea=2519.88 J⇒Ea=2520 J

options: Catalyst does not alter the Gibbs energy change of a reaction.
Catalyst can cause non-spontaneous reactions to occur.
The enthalpy change for the reaction is $+20 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
The enthalpy change for the catalysed reaction is different from that of uncatalysed reaction.
solution:
$\mathrm{A}_2+\mathrm{B}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{AB}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{f}}=180 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{b}}=200 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$\Delta \mathrm{H}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{b}}=-20 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
In presence of catalyst :
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{f}}=180-100=80 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
& \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{b}}=200-100=100 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$
Catalyst does not change $\Delta \mathrm{H}$ or $\Delta \mathrm{G}$ of a reaction.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
For a first-order reaction, rate = $k[A]$, where rate is directly proportional to reactant concentration.
Run 1: 100 mL of 10 M solution
Concentration = 10 M
Rate: $r_1 = k \times 10$
Run 2: 200 mL of 10 M solution
Concentration remains 10 M (increasing volume doesn't change concentration)
Rate: $r_2 = k \times 10$
Therefore $r_1 = r_2$
Run 3: 100 mL of 10 M solution + 100 mL of H₂O
Total moles of A = 10 M × 0.1 L = 1 mol
Total volume = 0.2 L
New concentration = 1 mol / 0.2 L = 5 M
Rate: $r_3 = k \times 5$
Therefore $r_3 < r_1 = r_2$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Activity $\left(-\frac{\mathrm{dN}}{\mathrm{dt}}\right) \propto \mathrm{N}$
$\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}_{\circ}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{n}}$
$\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{N}_{\circ}}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{n}}$
$\frac{1}{10}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{n}} \Rightarrow 10=2^{\mathrm{n}}$
$\log 10=\mathrm{nlog} 2$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=\frac{1}{0.301}=3.32$
$\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{n} \times \mathrm{t}_{112}$
$=3.32 \times 30=99.6 \text { days }$
Hence, (D) is correct.
